content:
I just had a few questions that I was hoping could be cleared up:

1. Given the group nature of the project, are individuals meant to be assigned to specific sections of text or should the volunteers work it out between themselves? I'm primarily worried about overlap in coverage if two people begin working on the same text before either of them uploads their work. 

2. Seeing as the Github upload guide mentions a naming scheme of "[Language]\_[Content]\_[Type].csv", are we expected to fully finish an entire character story/story episode/etc before uploading, or is it preferred that we upload whatever is done before patches even if incompleted?

3. To what extent does proofreading cover? I have been a part of various translation projects in the past and "proofreader" as a role has spanned a vast variety of responsibilities - anywhere from proofreading in the strictest literal sense (checking for punctuation errors, misspellings, grammar mistakes, etc, with no text editing privileges), to light editors (smoothing out awkward wording, rewriting unclear sentences, etc), to full-blown aggressive editors who are expected to rewrite the translator's output to match the expected voice and tone. Given the lack of an editor role, I presume it leans more towards the latter than the former, but editing is an entire separate skillset in and of itself and the style guide currently lacks any detailed literary editing guidelines (tone, voice, style, reading level, rhythm/flow, level of prose, etc)

4. Is there a differentiation in the files between currently human translated text and MTL which is pending human translation? I ask primarily as any time spent on editing/proofreading MTL text would be wasted effort if it gets overwritten by a human translator down the line

4. Is there a marker or designation for "finalized" text which should not be touched outside of strict proofreading (i.e., only fixing punctuation/spelling mistakes, with no text editing)? It would be good to know what text is considered "done" by the official staff so that we don't need to work on it any further

5. Are there any options available for testing in-game content that is not accessible or longer available? Stuff like past event stories, event UI, character birthday mails which may not have been sent out yet, warnings or notifications which might not come up in day-to-day play, etc? Similarly, is there a dev server or any method to test content that we may not necessarily have (specific wheels, character lore/quotes that are locked behind high affections or enlightens, later story or higher level content that testers might not have reached, etc)? 

6. In the same vein, is there a way for us to test changes ourselves without waiting for the changes to be pushed live via a patch update? For example, in a previous mobile game translation project I did, I tested the translations via unpacking the game resource files and repacking with a modified locale file which contained the edited text strings in order to view them ingame. I'm unsure if there are any officially supported methods for Morimens that would allow us to test changes client-side before they are pushed live.

Hey there,

Thanks so much for reaching out and sharing your questions! I really appreciate your enthusiasm and detailed suggestions. It's clear how much you care about the project's quality, and I want you to know I share that same passion. Your questions bring up a lot of things I've thought about myself, especially coming from larger teams with more resources.

I'm going to do my best to address your questions and give you a clearer picture of where we're at.

**Regarding Task Management and Tools**

**1. Assigning tasks and avoiding overlaps:**
Yes, for now, we have to lean on you all to **work it out among yourselves.** With our current limited staff (me, alone), it's just not possible for us to manage task assignments for every volunteer. We're incredibly grateful for any contribution, big or small, that you make. We're relying on volunteer **Moderators** for each language to help guide these efforts and keep things organized.

**2. Is there a minimum contribution?**
Absolutely not! You are **not expected to finish an entire chapter** when you upload a file to Github. We value any and all contributions, no matter how small. You can submit your work at any time. The naming template for files is just so our team can more easily see what each file is about.

**3. About Proofreading extent:**
Proofreading for this project simply means** editing the existing machine-translated text**. Yes. 
I know you're aiming for a high-quality, consistent output, and I truly appreciate that. To be blunt, we just don't have the resources or time right now to create a more detailed style guide or assign people to more specific roles. 
I'm fully aware this might lead to some inconsistencies, and I've talked to Light about it. He's completely on board because we see any contribution as an improvement. 

While we'd love to have a more professional setup, we're making the best of what we have. 
The good news is that we've been making more money lately, and Light said we'll soon be able to hire another person to help me manage and coordinate the volunteer project.

**4. A feature to avoid contribution being overwritten?**
I know exactly what you're talking about! There are professional TMS tools that show who's working on what in real-time, such as Phrase. Unfortunately, we don't have the resources for those yet. My previous team did. Morimens is just too broke now... 
When I compile volunteer contributions, I will double check with you if I find two volunteers uploading the same strings.
As an alternative, I can upload **a separate file for the English team that contains only the already human-translated text for reference.**

**5. Marker existing translations:**
Yes, I do have a file containing the 60,000 rows of text our team has worked on so far, and I can make that available.

**6. About how to test in-game content that are not available:**
Yes, we do have one.  It's a special GM testing tool for official, full-time staff, and to use it would technically require you to physically be here with us, so we can't make it available to volunteers. However, Light had an idea to add a new tool to the official app that would let you see the **multilanguage key** in-game. It's not exactly the same, but it would at least be something!


Your dedication means the world to us. While we can't provide all the professional resources you've asked for, we are incredibly grateful for anything you can contribute. Your work, even if it feels small, makes a huge difference.

Thank you again for your patience and support. Let me know if you have any more questions!

Some of the cells contain true line feeds, which kind of fucks up the export to .csv, whereas some other cells use \n in plain text to mark line feeds, and convert properly.

Is there a difference that means we should keep that (possibly meaning we can't submit .csv files for those), or is this just the result of technical constraints and we should prefer \n where needed ?

What is the protocol for words that are the same as realms? They occasionally show up in the text, and I can't tell if it's a conscious design decision or the result of an overzealous find+replace.

Reference:
血肉 is the term for "Caro", but also just means flesh/body. There's multiple times in the story where something happens (someone is stabbed, they are wounded, etc) where the English text uses the word "Caro" in place of blood, body, or flesh. I'm unsure if this should stay or if it's meant to be blood/flesh in the appropriate contexts.
**__Examples:__** 
"Do you know what it feels like to be torn apart by a wolf? Let me tell you, it's the pain of sharp teeth piercing deeply, then whole chunks of Caro and nerves being ripped away—"
"The magic circle opens its gaping maw, devouring his Caro bit by bit until the wizard is reduced to nothing but an empty skeleton."
"Bones crack, Caro tears, and Sophia's Body twists and transforms before you. Her hands elongate, claws replace fingers, and her hind legs bend, standing like a canine in a pool of blood."

深海 is the term for Aequor, but also just literally means deep sea. Looking at the term list, this is generally translated as some form of "deep sea" or "abyss", which means it's being used in the literal sense and not as a realm reference. There are cases of the word "Aequor" being used in english text, though, which is what confuses me since it's clearly referring to the deep sea in context but the english text says Aequor and I can't tell if that's a deliberate choice or not.
**__Examples:__** 
"Casual Dimension travel is like diving 10,000 meters deep into Aequor without protection or climbing the snowy, stormy Elworth peaks in just a shirt."
"It's as scorching as the sun, yet as dark and cold as the Aequor."
"—You fall into the Aequor."
"Was the last force that helped me Celeste? She pulled me out of Aequor..."

Ultra doesn't seem to have any instances of it since it's a fairly unique phrase and all instances of it I found were direct references to either ultra awakeners or the realm mechanic

Chaos doesn't seem to suffer at all from this because the realm is named the same as the word so there's no conflict there
I would like to add the this is very weird as a reader, and something I'd instinctively consider a typo/error, so I hope that this isn't a subject that's too dear to the narrative team.

Short answer: these terms should be kept in imo, as prose.
Long answer:
An important part of the writing of this genre is the invocation of fanciful language to represent or describe things in the lore that imply more mystique than what can normally be found in the real world. The careful implementation of the distinction between **Esoteric language** and **Pedestrian language** in Lovecraftian writing is one of the primary techniques that brings the genre the *fascination* it's known so well for.

Let's take **Caro** as an example. You and I both fully understand what "Flesh" means. We know what bones, nerves, tendons, muscles, blood, etc. are because these are pedestrian terms. Sure, we don't use some of these words every day, so reading them being twisted is cool sometimes but the fact that we fully understand what these things are means that when we read about "whole chunks of muscle and nerves being ripped away—" we're instantly done compartmentalizing what that means and there's nothing left to the imagination.

However, the use of the esoteric language "Caro" in this case brings a sense of "I don't know *exactly* what that means" and the thought can linger in the back of the mind. It brings more impact to the sentence as Caro isn't just *flesh*, it represents some blend of *flesh* and the *essence of flesh-ness*, both real and spiritual.

Can you as a human survive the *thorough flaying of your body*? **No.**
Can you as a human survive the *thorough flaying of your Caro*? I dunno, **maybe?** What does it *mean* to *survive* that?

That being said, I have seen some of these terms used when they seem like they shouldn't, and that should be considered in-context on a case-by-case basis by an editor that understands when it's most impactful to use esoteric language, but in most cases I've read, it's been implemented pretty well, so I wouldn't change that if you're unsure.

I'd like to mention this all applies diegetically as well. A vast majority of the people in the story's world have *no idea whatsoever* what Caro, Aequor, Dissolution, Silver, Awakeners, Dimensions, Domains etc are. Mythag University would have no unique advantage if normal people knew what Silver was. Daffodil would have no business model whatsoever if normal people knew a single thing about literally anything she deals in.

So yes, while Aequor does literally mean "deep sea", there's an Esoteric term and a Pedestrian term at play here, and that distinction should always be accounted for when assessing the prose of a statement.

thanks for coming to my ted talk
